content:
Actually, the common belief that porcupines can shoot their quills at predators is a myth. Porcupines do not have the ability to launch or shoot their quills. Instead, their quills are loosely attached to their skin and can easily detach when a predator comes into contact with them.

When threatened, a porcupine will often raise and fan out its quills to appear larger and more intimidating. If a predator gets too close and makes contact, the quills will embed into the predator's skin. The barbed quills are difficult and painful to remove, serving as a strong defense mechanism.

So, to clarify, porcupines defend themselves by using their quills as a passive weapon that detaches upon contact, not by actively shooting them.
